coredata.dc:title: MnO2 nanosheets-based colorimetric sensor for uric acid detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shu, Xin
Huang, Li
Guo, Xiaoyin
Shi, Rui
Qin, Shangying
Wang, Yilin
coredata.dc:description: Serum uric acid (UA) content is an important reference indicator for diagnosing diseases. In the present work, MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) were synthesized, as well as an enzyme-free colorimetric method for sensitive determination of UA was developed. It was based on the strong oxidation capacity of MnO2 NSs, which could oxidize colorless o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to generate yellow product diaminophenazine (oxOPD) with an absorption peak at 420 nm. However, the addition of UA caused the structure of MnO2 NSs to be destroyed and reduced to Mn2+, in turn causing a decrease in the efficiency of oxidation and absorbance intensity. Further investigation indicated that the decrease in absorbance increased with the increase of UA concentration. Under the optimized conditions of pH 7.4, temperature 60 °C, incubation time 50 min, and MnO2 NSs 20 μM, the proposed method displayed a detection limit of 0.87 μM and a linear response range of 1.0–90 μM, making its detection of UA simple, fast, and sensitive. Furthermore, the achieved good recovery (95.3 %–102.8 %) in serum samples confirmed the potential of the developed method for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colorimetry
Determination
Uric acid
MnO2 nanosheets

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: MnO2 NSs-mediated oxidation of OPD in the absence and presence of UA
The most favorable conditions for UA detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of MnO2 NSs (A) TEM image; Inset: UV–vis absorption spectra; (B) XPS spectrum; XPS spectra of (C) Mn 2p and (D) O 1s; (E) XRD pattern.
(A) Absorption spectra of different reaction systems; (B) TEM image of MnO2 NSs in the presence of UA.
Effects of (A) buffer solution pH, (B) temperature, (C) time, (D) MnO2 NSs concentration on the reaction.
(A) Effect of UA concentration on absorbance, (B) linear equation between [UA] and ΔA.

The effect of interferents on the reaction system. The concentrations of (a) urea, (b) lysine, (c) rhamnose, (d) magnesium chloride, (e) calcium carbonate, (f) fructose, (g) Melamine, (h) lactose, (i) sodium chloride, and (j) glucose, are all 1 mM. The concentration of (k) uric acid is 50 μM.
Principle of colorimetric method for UA determination.
Performance comparison of different materials and methods for UA detection.
Determination results of UA in human serum.

head.ce:title: MnO2 nanosheets-based colorimetric sensor for uric acid detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Serum uric acid (UA) content is an important reference indicator for diagnosing diseases. In the present work, MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) were synthesized, as well as an enzyme-free colorimetric method for sensitive determination of UA was developed. It was based on the strong oxidation capacity of MnO2 NSs, which could oxidize colorless o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to generate yellow product diaminophenazine (oxOPD) with an absorption peak at 420 nm. However, the addition of UA caused the structure of MnO2 NSs to be destroyed and reduced to Mn2+, in turn causing a decrease in the efficiency of oxidation and absorbance intensity. Further investigation indicated that the decrease in absorbance increased with the increase of UA concentration. Under the optimized conditions of pH 7.4, temperature 60 °C, incubation time 50 min, and MnO2 NSs 20 μM, the proposed method displayed a detection limit of 0.87 μM and a linear response range of 1.0–90 μM, making its detection of UA simple, fast, and sensitive. Furthermore, the achieved good recovery (95.3 %–102.8 %) in serum samples confirmed the potential of the developed method for practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All relevant data from the study are available within the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (UA) is the final product of purine compounds catabolism in human body. Normal UA level is conducive to energy storage, but excessive content is easy to induce gout. In addition, UA levels are closely associated with various diseases [1,2]. Therefore, the quantitative determination of UA in body fluids is of great significance for disease diagnosis. At present, the main methods for determining UA include chromatography [3], electrochemical [4], fluorescence [5] and colorimetry [6,7]. Without the help of expensive instruments, colorimetric method is convenient and can even be easily monitored by the naked eyes [8–11]. The principle of colorimetry is based on the tandem catalysis of uricase and horseradish peroxidase (HRP). Under the catalysis of uricase, UA is oxidized to produce allantoin and H2O2, then the generated H2O2 oxidizes 3, 3′, 5, 5′- tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) or O-phenylenediamine (OPD) to produce colored oxidation products under the catalysis of HRP, which resulting color changes in the solution and corresponding changes in absorbance values. The UA content can be observed by naked eye or calculated by detecting the change of absorbance. In this process, two natural enzymes (uricase and HRP) are indispensable. Natural enzymes can catalyze and accelerate chemical reactions efficiently and selectively, but their practical applications are limited due to their low stability and high cost [12,13]. In recent years, some artificial simulated enzymes have been explored as an alternative of HRP. Zhang et al. [14] established a colorimetric/fluorescent probe based on the oxidization of OPD with iodine ions instead of HRP, achieving dual signal output for UA recognition in 2019. He et al. [15] used functionalized multiple quantum dots (GSH-Ti3C2 MQDs), while Wang et al. [16] adopted carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to achieve colorimetric/fluorescence ratio detection of UA through the uricase-QDs tandem catalysis. More substances replacing HRP, such as Cu2+ [7], Ag3PO4 nanoparticles [17], metal organic frameworks (Th-MOF) [6] and MoS2 [18], have also successfully accomplished the determination of UA in TMB system. Obviously, the probes reported were all designed based on the oxidation reaction of H2O2 to TMB or OPD, so uricase was still essential.

Manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NSs), as a typical two-dimensional layered transition metal oxide, have attracted much attention due to its wide absorption band (200–600 nm), excellent absorption capacity, excellent reversible redox capacity and biocompatibility [19,20]. Mn4+ is the intermediate valence state of manganese with MnO2/Mn2+ standard hydrogen electrode potential of 1.23 V. On the one hand, the strong oxidizing ability of MnO2 NSs can oxidize multiple substances, including OPD [21], TMB [22,23] and methylene blue [24]. Therefore, MnO2 NSs with strong oxidation ability, easy preparation and high stability become good materials for replacing H2O2-enzyme combination. On the other hand, MnO2 NSs can be easily reduced to Mn2+ by reductive substances, in turn causing a decrease in the efficiency of oxidation and absorbance intensity. Based on this principle, many antioxidants have been determined [25–28]. In the absence of any other nanomaterials but MnO2 NSs, Huang et al. [29] established a colorimetric sensing method of glutathione (GSH). Wang et al. [30] reported a sensing platform for colorimetric detection of acid phosphatase (ACP) and screening of ACP inhibitors using MnO2 NSs. Due to its high molar extinction coefficient and strong oxidation ability, MnO2 NSs can also be used to construct colorimetric/fluorescent dual signal sensing platforms. The addition of MnO2 NSs quench the fluorescence of curcumin (CUR), and the color of the mixture changes from yellow to brown. In the presence of biothiols, MnO2 NSs are reduced and lose their fluorescence quenching ability, resulting in an increase in the fluorescence of CUR at 540 nm and a decrease in the absorbance at 430 nm. Thus, a dual‑channel sensor array fordiscrimination ofbiothiols was reported by Leng et al. [31]. In this work, an enzyme-free colorimetric method for sensitive detection of UA based on the strong oxidation ability of MnO2 NSs was presented. As illuminated in Scheme 1 , the yellow product oxOPD with absorption peaks at 420 nm was oxidized from colorless OPD by MnO2 NSs. The involvement of UA disrupts MnO2 NSs structure and reduces them to Mn2+, in turn causing a decrease in the efficiency of oxidation and absorbance intensity. The color of the reaction and the decrease in absorbance exhibited a good linear relationship with UA concentration, thus achieving UA determination and successfully applied to the detection in human serum. The prominent advantage of this method is its cost-effectiveness, as it does not use any natural or artificial enzymes. As far as we know, the enzyme-free determination of UA was firstly reported using OPD as the chromogenic substrate.

UA, OPD and tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMA·OH) were provided by Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD (Shanghai, China). Manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium chloride (NaCl), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), potassium dihydrogen phosphate, urea, trichloroacetic acid (TCA), glucose (Glu), and lysine (Lys) were supplied by Damao Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Melamine, rhamnose, fructose, lactose and disodium hydrogen phosphate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical reagent Co., LTD (Shanghai, China). Double distilled water (DDW) was used and all reagents have not been purified furtherly.

The absorbance spectrum was obtained by the Unico UV-4802 double beam ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai Uniko Instrument Co., LTD). TEM and XPS were completed by Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN (FEI, USA) and Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) respectively. An H1850 high speed centrifuge (Changsha Xiangyi) was used for centrifugal operations. A pHS-3E precision pH meter (Shanghai Leici) was used for adjusting the pH values. An HH-S2 thermostatic water bath (Jintan Medical Instrument Factory) was used to keep track of the temperature.

The synthesis of MnO2 NSs was carried out according to reference [32], which involved the reaction of TMA·OH and H2O2 to oxidize MnCl2·4H2O. In brief, 10 mL of MnCl2 (0.3 M) was transferred to a 20 mL of TMA·OH (0.6 M) and H2O2 (3 %) in their mixture. After continuous stirring at room temperature for 12 h, the resulting suspension was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min), and the precipitate was washed with double deionized water (DDW) and methanol three times, respectively. Subsequently, the purified MnO2 NSs was redispersed in DDW, and an ultrasonic treatment was performed to prepare a uniform MnO2 NSs stock solution. The accurate concentration of MnO2 NSs can be calculated by Lamber-Beer law, as its molar absorption coefficient is 9.6 × 103 L·mol−1·cm−1 at 380 nm.

Series of test solutions were prepared by well mixing 700 μL of 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4), 100 μL of UA with different concentrations, 100 μL of MnO2 NSs (200 μM), and 100 μL of OPD (2.0 mM). After incubation at 60 °C for 50 min, the UV–vis absorption spectrum corresponding to different UA concentrations was measured, and the absorption value at 420 nm was recorded. The decrease in absorbance at 420 nm is represented by ΔA for standard curve and interference experiments, while the relative absorbance (ΔA/A0 ) is used to evaluate the condition experiments. ΔA = A0 − A, where A0 and A represent absorbance without and with UA, respectively. Three parallel measurements were used in all experiments.

Several characterization tests were used to prove the successful synthesis of MnO2 NSs. The UV–visible absorption spectrum (inset in Fig. 1 A) exhibits a wide absorption band with a center wavelength of 380 nm that arises from the d-d transition of Mn (IV) in the octahedral [MnO6] unit [32]. The MnO2 NSs suspension exhibited good stability, even after being stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 2 weeks, its absorption peak position and absorbance measurements are very consistent with previous measurements. TEM images (Fig. 1A) were used to observe the surface morphology of MnO2 NSs, and it can be clearly seen that the prepared MnO2 NSs have a wrinkled, thin two-dimensional sheet-like structure. The special nanostructure provides a large specific surface area for MnO2 NSs, which is conducive to the full and rapid reaction of substances such as UA. XPS was used to demonstrate the chemical element and its corresponding valence state. In Fig. 1B, there is a very strong signal at the binding energy positions corresponding to Mn, O, and C, indicating the presence of Mn, O, and C elements in the substance. As shown in Fig. 1C, the high-resolution XPS spectra of Mn 2p show two peaks (641.58 and 653.38 eV), which can be attributed to Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 orbitals. There are also details in the fitting of the two main peaks of MnO2, with two peaks (653.68 and 643.08 eV) belonging to Mn4+. Additionally, the peaks centered around 652.78 and 641.48 eV are oxygen vacancies near Mn4+ [29,33]. Fig. 1D shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s with three different peaks at 528.88, 530.28, and 531.98 eV, respectively belonging to Mn–O, C = O, and C–O, which are consistent with the reported structure of MnO2 NSs in the literature [34]. In the XRD pattern (Fig. 1E), the four characteristic peaks are located at 18.8°, 27.8°, 36.6°, and 65.4°, which are assigned to the (002), (003), (100) and (110) reflection of the layered MnO2 [2]. In summary, MnO2 with nanosheet structure was successfully synthesized in the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MnO2 NSs-mediated oxidation of OPD in the absence and presence of UA
ce:section.ce:para: Controlled experiments were conducted for exploring the feasibility of the colorimetric method, by measuring the absorbance of OPD in different systems. From Fig. 2 A, it can be clearly seen that OPD solution has no absorption in 350–550 nm range. Meanwhile, MnO2 NSs contained OPD exhibits a distinct absorption peak at 420 nm due to the formation of oxidation product oxOPD. Adding UA to OPD solution containing MnO2 NSs leads to a decrease in absorbance, indicating a reduction in the generation of oxOPD. Compared with the original morphology of MnO2 NSs (Fig. 1A), the morphology of MnO2 NSs in the presence of UA is fragmented (Fig. 2B). Implying that the MnO2 have be reduced to Mn2+ by UA. These results indicate that the oxidation of OPD with MnO2 NSs would be inhibited by UA, causing a decrease in absorbance related to the concentration of UA. Therefore, UA can be quantitatively detected by measuring the decrease of absorbance, and the chemical reaction of this process can be described as follows:

ce:section.ce:section-title: The most favorable conditions for UA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The conditions were optimized for factors that may affect the experimental results, such as pH value, temperature, incubation time, and concentration of MnO2 NSs. Considering the fact that H+ ions participate in the redox reactions related to MnO2 and the solubility of UA increases with the increase of pH, the influence of pH on UA detection was studied by using PBS with different pH values (5.8–8.6). As displayed in Fig. 3 A, with the increase of pH, the value of ΔA/A0 increases gradually. Whereas the pH exceeds 7.4, the variation pattern of ΔA/A0 value is exactly the opposite. Thus, pH 7.4 was chosen for the next investigations. The effect of reaction temperature on UA detection was investigated in the range of 35–75 °C, and the results are shown in Fig. 3B. As can be seen, lower reaction temperature (35–60 °C) leads to slower reaction speed and smaller ΔA/A0 value. On the other hand, a reaction temperature exceeding 60 °C can also lead to a decrease in ΔA/A0 value. It is clear that the maximum value of ΔA/A0 is attained at the reaction temperature of 60 °C. In subsequent experiments, 60 °C was selected as the reaction temperature. The influence of various incubation time (10–80 min) on UA detection was also examined. As exhibited in Fig. 3C, the ΔA/A0 value increases with the prolongation of incubation time and eventually reaches basic stability. In this work, 50 min was chosen as the best incubation time. In OPD-MnO2 NSs systems at the same OPD concentration, the lower the concentration of MnO2 NSs, the less oxidation of OPD, the lower the absorbance, and the narrower the linear response range. Although the higher the concentration of MnO2 NSs, the wider the linear response range, excessive MnO2 NSs may lead to low sensitivity. As shown in Fig. 3D, the maximum ΔA/A0 is obtained at 20 μM of MnO2 NSs. Thus, a probe solution containing 200 μM OPD and 20 μM MnO2 NSs in their mixture was selected for UA detection.

Based on the oxidation capacity of MnO2 NSs, the colorimetric determination of UA was carried out under the optimal conditions. Different concentrations of UA in the range of 0–90 μM were added to detect the absorbance at 420 nm. Fig. 4 A shows a gradual decrease in absorbance as the concentration of UA increases. Moreover, when the concentration of UA ranges from 1.0 to 90 μM, ΔA presents a good linear relationship with the concentration of UA (Fig. 4B). The linear equation is ΔA = 0.0017[UA] + 0.0016, and the correlation coefficient is R2 = 0.996. According to the 3σ/slope rule, the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.87 μM, showing high sensitivity for UA assay. The relative standard deviations (RSD) for the five repeated tests of 5 μM, 30 μM, and 70 μM UA were 2.6 %, 0.8 %, and 0.9 % respectively, indicating a good precision of the method. Table 1 lists the results of comparing this method with others from different materials. It can be seen that the linear range in this work is wider while the LOD is lower than most of others, which fully indicates that the colorimetric method has good performance and is suitable for the detection of UA.

In the process of UA detection, some substances may cause interference to the experiment, resulting in a decline in the accuracy. In order to study the selectivity, some organic matter and common chemical substances that may exist in the human body are added to the reaction system. These include urea, lysine, melamine, rhamnose, lactose, fructose, glucose, Na+, Ca2+ and Mg2+. All the above-mentioned substances were configured into a solution of 1 mM and the experiment was carried out under the best conditions. The result in Fig. 5 shows that even if the concentration of the added disruptor is 20 times that of UA, it has little effect on the reaction system and represents a high selectivity. Suggesting that the OPD-MnO2 NSs probe can be applied for determining UA in real samples.

In order to investigate the clinical potential of this colorimetric method, experiments were conducted to detect human serum samples which were obtained from Guangxi University Hospital of three healthy adults. The serum samples were diluted 10 times with PBS buffer solution at pH 7.4, and 100 μL was taken for the above experiment as 2.3. Three parallel experiments were carried out. The results are shown in Table 2 . Considering a dilution of 100 times, the original UA concentrations in the samples were 234, 330 and 324 μM, respectively, which were all within the range of normal human serum UA content from 120 to 460 μM [40]. In addition, the accuracy of the method was verified by standard addition method. The recoveries were in the range of 95.3 %–102.8 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was between 1.3 % and 4.5 %. The results show that the colorimetric method has high reliability and accuracy, making a great potentiality in clinical detection.

An enzyme-free colorimetric strategy for sensitive and selective determination of UA based on MnO2-OPD sensing platform has been constructed. The detection mechanism is based on the oxidation properties of MnO2 NSs, which can oxidize colorless OPD to generate yellow oxOPD, accompanied by changes in colorimetric signals. However, MnO2 NSs can be quickly reduced to Mn2+ with the introduction of UA, resulting in a decrease in the absorbance at 420 nm. The change in absorbance shows a good linear relationship with the concentration of UA. It exhibits good performance with wide linear range of 1.0–90 μM and low detection limit of 0.87 μM. Furthermore, this method has been successfully applied to the determination of UA in human serum, further indicating the potential application of MnO2 NSs in clinical diagnosis. Although not suitable for continuous and in situ analysis, the method is low-cost and convenient popularization as it does not involve any enzymes and expensive instruments.

Xin Shu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Li Huang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Xiaoyin Guo: Investigation, Data curation. Rui Shi: Validation. Shangying Qin: Project administration. Yilin Wang: Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (YCSW2023049) for their financial support.
